module.title: Genre: Lo-Fi
module.description: Create the warm, nostalgic sound of lo-fi hip hop. Learn vinyl texture, dusty drum programming, jazz harmony, and the mixing techniques that give lo-fi its signature mellow, imperfect character.

lessons.title: The Lo-Fi Sound: Vinyl Texture and Warmth
lessons.body:
Lo-fi hip hop is defined by its imperfections. The crackle of a worn vinyl record, the warmth of an overdriven tape machine, the slightly off-pitch piano of a cheap Casio — these sonic artifacts don't ruin the music, they *are* the music. Understanding why these imperfections are appealing, and how to recreate them deliberately, is the foundation of the lo-fi aesthetic.

## Why Imperfection Sounds Good

Perfect digital audio is clean, flat, and transient-accurate. It's also emotionally cold. Analog hardware — vinyl records, tape machines, tube amplifiers — introduces non-linear distortion, frequency response curves, noise floors, and mechanical resonances. These imperfections are consistent with how our ears have learned to associate sound quality with *recorded music history*. They trigger nostalgia and warmth.

Lo-fi producers intentionally layer these imperfections:

1. **Vinyl noise** — crackle, pops, surface hiss
2. **Tape saturation** — harmonic distortion from overdriving tape heads
3. **Wow and flutter** — subtle pitch modulation from a warped or worn record
4. **Bit reduction** — lower bit-depth quantisation noise
5. **Vinyl EQ** — the RIAA curve that rolls off bass and high frequency response

## Adding Vinyl Texture in FL Studio

**Method 1 — Sample a vinyl noise loop**: Download a free vinyl crackle recording (royalty-free sources include Freesound.org). Load it into a Sampler channel, loop it, and set it to play continuously throughout the track. Volume: keep it low — -18 to -24 dB below the main elements. The crackle should be felt rather than heard consciously.

**Method 2 — Fruity Convolver (convolution reverb)**: Load a vinyl impulse response. Convolvers can apply the frequency response signature of a specific vinyl pressing or recording space. This is a more subtle approach that adds the EQ character of vinyl without adding noise.

**Method 3 — Vinyl Distortion simulation**: Use **Fruity Blood Overdrive** with a subtle drive setting (10–20%) to add harmonic saturation that mimics overdriven tube or tape. Set the tone control to round off the high end slightly.

## Tape Saturation

Tape saturation has two effects: **compression** (loud signals are compressed naturally by the magnetic saturation point) and **harmonic generation** (even-order harmonics added, which sound warm and musical rather than harsh). In FL Studio:

- **Fruity Waveshaper**: Set a gentle soft-clip curve. This is the simplest tape saturation simulation.
- **Parametric EQ 2 + drive**: Use the preamp drive knob (the small dial at the left of Parametric EQ 2) to push the signal into mild saturation before EQ filtering.
- **Third-party plugins**: If you have Izotope Vinyl, Caelum Audio Tape Cassette, or similar, they are purpose-built for this effect.

## Wow and Flutter (Pitch Wobble)

Wow is slow pitch variation (from a warped record); flutter is faster pitch variation (from turntable motor inconsistency). Together they make the pitch feel slightly alive and unstable.

In FL Studio, recreate this with an **LFO on pitch**:
1. On the piano or instrument channel, open the channel properties
2. Go to the INS (instrument) knob section
3. Right-click the Pitch knob → Create automation clip
4. In the automation clip, draw a subtle sine wave with very low amplitude (pitch range ±3–8 cents) and a slow speed (0.2–0.5 Hz for wow, 5–8 Hz for flutter)
5. Alternatively, use the **Patcher** or a dedicated plugin to apply pitch modulation

A combined wow+flutter LFO uses a slow sine for the wow component and a slightly faster, lower-amplitude irregular curve for flutter. The irregularity is key — perfect sine waves sound artificial.

## Bit Crushing and Sample Rate Reduction

Reducing the bit depth (from 24-bit to 8–12-bit) adds a granular, crunchy noise floor. Reducing the sample rate (from 44.1 kHz to 11–22 kHz) adds aliasing artefacts and dulls the high frequency response. In FL Studio:

- **Fruity Blood Overdrive** at low settings can simulate some bit reduction character
- **Gross Beat** can do buffer effects that include bit-reduction style artefacts
- The dedicated tool is **Fruity Bits Crusher** (in the Effects browser). Set:
  - **Bits**: 12 (moderate crunch) to 8 (heavy lofi texture)
  - **Freq**: 22 kHz (subtle) to 11 kHz (strong vintage character)
  - **Mix**: 30–60% so the crushed signal blends with the clean signal

## The Lo-Fi EQ Signature

Lo-fi music has a specific frequency character:

- **Rolled-off high end**: Nothing above 12–14 kHz. Use a high-shelf cut at 10 kHz, -3 to -6 dB. This immediately sounds vintage.
- **Rolled-off sub bass**: High-pass at 40–50 Hz. Lo-fi hip hop doesn't have sub-heavy bass — the low end is warm mid-bass rather than deep sub.
- **Presence in 1–3 kHz**: A gentle boost here adds intimacy and a "mono speaker" character.
- **Warmth at 200–400 Hz**: A subtle boost in this range adds the fullness of a physically small speaker.

Applying this EQ curve to the entire mix bus (using Parametric EQ 2 on the master) is a fast way to impose a lo-fi character on an otherwise modern-sounding production.
keyTakeaways: Vinyl crackle, tape saturation, wow/flutter, and bit crushing are the four main lo-fi texture tools — use all of them at low levels simultaneously rather than one at high intensity.
The lo-fi EQ signature rolls off high frequencies above 12–14 kHz and warms the 200–400 Hz range — applying this to the master bus instantly gives a vintage character.
Imperfections should be felt rather than consciously heard — if you can clearly identify the crackle or the pitch wobble, it's too loud.
lessons.tryThis: Load a vinyl crackle sample (any free loop from the internet) into a Sampler channel. Loop it and set volume to -22 dB. Now add Fruity Bits Crusher to your main melody channel — set Bits to 12 and Freq to 22 kHz, mix at 40%. Finally, add a Parametric EQ 2 to your master bus and apply: high-shelf cut at 10 kHz (-4 dB), low-shelf cut at 50 Hz (-3 dB), and a gentle boost at 250 Hz (+2 dB). Play any melody you have through this chain and compare with it bypassed — that's the lo-fi signature EQ at work.

lessons.title: Lo-Fi Drum Programming
lessons.body:
Lo-fi drums are the opposite of trap drums in almost every way. Where trap hits hard and bright with clinical precision, lo-fi hits soft and muffled with human imperfection. The drums should feel like a jazz drummer brushing a snare in a small room, not like samples triggered at 127 velocity in a sterile studio.

## The Lo-Fi Drum Palette

Sample choice is everything in lo-fi drum programming. The key characteristics:

- **Kick**: Soft, short, pillowy. No sharp click transient. A jazz kick or a 60s soul record kick works well. The kick should feel like a thump, not a punch.
- **Snare**: Warm, slightly muffled, with a natural room decay. Brush snare hits or rim hits are common. A bright trap snare sounds completely wrong in a lo-fi context.
- **Hi-hats**: Slightly muffled, with a loose, airy sound. Real hi-hat recordings from jazz or soul records are ideal. The hat should breathe rather than click.
- **Percussion**: Congas, bongos, shakers, tambourines — lo-fi percussion adds organic texture. These often come from sampled breaks.

**Sampling breaks**: Many lo-fi producers sample drum breaks from jazz and soul records, then chop them up. The timing irregularities and room acoustics of a real drum recording are difficult to replicate from scratch. Even a single snare hit or hi-hat tap from a vinyl record has a character that programmed samples can't fully match.

## Programming the Pattern

Lo-fi drum patterns are based on classic hip-hop boom bap — but slower (75–95 BPM) and with more swing.

**Basic lo-fi pattern (1 bar, 1/16 steps)**:

```
Kick:   [1] . . . . . [2.5] . [3] . . . . . . .
Snare:  . . . . [2] . . . . . . . [4] . . . .
Hi-hat: [1] . [+] . [2] . [+] . [3] . [+] . [4] . [+] .
```

The hi-hat plays straight 8th notes (every other 16th step) as the foundation. The kick has a syncopated hit at beat 2.5 (the "and" of beat 2) — the classic boom-bap placement that makes the groove feel like it's pushing forward.

**Swing**: Apply swing to the hi-hat pattern. In the Step Sequencer, set swing to 20–35%. This offsets the even-8th notes so they sit slightly after the beat — the hallmark of boom-bap groove. Heavier swing values (above 40%) create a more pronounced shuffle that borders on jazz triplet feel.

## Ghost Notes: The Secret of Live Feel

The single element that separates a programmed pattern from a live-feeling one is ghost notes. Ghost snares are soft, barely audible notes (velocity 10–25) placed in between the main snare hits. They fill in the rhythmic spaces that a real drummer would naturally fill.

Programme ghost snares on:
- The 16th note before beat 2 (the anticipation)
- The 16th note after beat 2 (the aftermath)
- The "and" of beat 3 (mid-bar syncopation)

These notes should be nearly inaudible — if you can hear them distinctly, they're too loud. Their purpose is to create the sensation that the pattern is breathing.

## Kick Variation Across Bars

A real drummer doesn't play the identical kick pattern every bar. Vary the kick across 4-bar phrases:

- **Bar 1**: Standard pattern (beats 1 and 2.5)
- **Bar 2**: Add a second kick on beat 4.5 (the "and" of beat 4) — a push toward bar 3
- **Bar 3**: Repeat bar 1
- **Bar 4**: Drop the syncopated kick, add a double kick at beat 1 (beats 1 and 1.5) — a phrase-ending statement

This 4-bar variation creates a musical phrase structure in the drums, not just a looping 1-bar pattern.

## Processing Lo-Fi Drums

The processing approach is almost the opposite of trap:

**Compression**: Use a warm-sounding compressor (VCA character, moderate attack 20–40 ms). The goal is glue and warmth, not transient control. Aim for 3–4 dB GR on the drum bus — enough to settle the dynamics without killing the life.

**EQ**: Rather than boosting presence and high end, cut it:
- High-pass kick at 40 Hz
- Gentle low-pass on the snare at 10–12 kHz — roll off the brightness
- Low-pass on the full drum bus at 14–16 kHz to remove digital harshness

**Room reverb**: A short room reverb (0.4–0.6s decay, 5 ms pre-delay) on the snare gives the sense of a small room. Keep it mono or narrow stereo.

**Vinyl/tape treatment**: Run the entire drum bus through the bit crusher and/or tape saturation from Lesson 1. Drums heavily benefit from vinyl texture because they're the most obviously "digital" element when using programmed samples.

**Pitch variation on the hi-hat**: Slightly detune individual hi-hat hits by ±5–15 cents using the Sampler's pitch settings on individual steps. This mimics the natural variation of a real drummer and is one of the most effective lo-fi humanisation techniques.

## The Chopped Break Approach

An alternative to programming from scratch: sample a drum break from a jazz or soul record, chop it in the Sampler or Edison, and rearrange the hits.

1. Find a drum break recording (ensure it's cleared or use royalty-free recordings)
2. Load into Edison → identify individual hits
3. Export each hit (kick, snare, hat) as a separate sample
4. Program using those authentic-sounding samples

The room acoustics and performance character of a real recording are embedded in these samples, giving an instant lo-fi feel that's hard to match with synthetic drum machines.
keyTakeaways: Lo-fi drums should be soft and muffled, not bright and punchy — choose samples with natural room decay and apply a low-pass filter to cut digital harshness above 12–14 kHz.
Swing (20–35%) applied to the hi-hat step sequencer transforms a straight pattern into the loose, human feel that defines boom-bap and lo-fi groove.
Ghost notes at velocity 10–25 on the snare fill rhythmic gaps and create a live, breathing feel — they're felt rather than consciously heard.
lessons.tryThis: Program a 4-bar lo-fi drum loop at 85 BPM. Set swing to 25% in the Step Sequencer. Add ghost snares at velocity 20 on the 16th note just before and after beats 2 and 4 in bars 1 and 3. Vary the kick on bar 4 as described above. Now run the entire drum bus through a Fruity Bits Crusher (Bits 14, Freq 22 kHz, mix 30%). A/B with and without the bit crusher — notice the subtle warmth and vintage character it adds.

lessons.title: Jazz Chords for Lo-Fi
lessons.body:
Jazz harmony is the heart of lo-fi hip hop. The extended chords — major 7ths, minor 9ths, dominant 13ths — give lo-fi its mellow, sophisticated emotional quality. You don't need to be a jazz pianist to use these chords in your productions. You need to understand a handful of chord types, how to voice them on the Piano Roll, and how they connect to create the lo-fi sound.

## Why Jazz Chords Sound Lo-Fi

Pop and contemporary music tends toward simple triads (3 notes) and power chords (2 notes). Jazz chords stack additional thirds on top of the basic triad, adding the 7th, 9th, 11th, and 13th degrees. These extensions create a richness and ambiguity — they resolve less definitively than simple chords, which gives them a suspended, contemplative quality that suits the lo-fi vibe.

Lo-fi hip hop borrows primarily from cool jazz and soul — not bebop (which is harmonically complex and rhythmically intense). The specific chord vocabulary:

| Chord Type | Formula (intervals) | Sound Character |
|---|---|---|
| Major 7 | 1 – 3 – 5 – 7 | Bright, dreamy, resolved |
| Minor 7 | 1 – b3 – 5 – b7 | Mellow, melancholic, versatile |
| Dominant 7 | 1 – 3 – 5 – b7 | Bluesy tension, wants to resolve |
| Major 9 | 1 – 3 – 5 – 7 – 9 | Lush, expansive, cinematic |
| Minor 9 | 1 – b3 – 5 – b7 – 9 | Soulful, yearning |
| Half-diminished (m7b5) | 1 – b3 – b5 – b7 | Melancholic tension, used as ii chord |
| Dominant 9 | 1 – 3 – 5 – b7 – 9 | Funky, soulful tension |

## Building Chord Voicings in the Piano Roll

A **voicing** is which specific octave you place each note of a chord. The same chord can sound vastly different depending on the voicing. Lo-fi jazz chords are typically voiced in a specific way:

**Close voicing** (all notes packed together): Sounds full but sometimes muddy. Good for the upper register.

**Open voicing** (notes spread across 2 octaves): Sounds spacious and clear. This is the preferred lo-fi voicing.

**Drop 2 voicing** (the second-highest note of a close voicing is dropped an octave): Creates a wide, open sound. Common in jazz piano and guitar.

**Example — C Major 7 in Drop 2**:
- Close voicing: C4 – E4 – G4 – B4
- Drop 2: C4 – G4 – B4 – E5 (the E has been dropped to below the G)

In the Piano Roll, enter chords by clicking multiple notes on the same time position. Hold **Ctrl** (or use chord mode) to enter multiple notes at once.

## Common Lo-Fi Chord Progressions

**The ii – V – I (jazz standard)**:
In C major: Dm7 → G7 → Cmaj7
This is the most fundamental jazz progression. The ii chord (minor 7) creates mild tension, the V chord (dominant 7) creates strong tension, and the I chord (major 7) resolves. Lo-fi productions often loop just the ii – V or ii – V – I endlessly — the resolution at Cmaj7 creates a satisfying, cyclical sense.

**The I – VI – II – V (turnaround)**:
In C major: Cmaj7 → Am7 → Dm7 → G7
A complete cycle through the key. Each chord borrows tension from the next. Very common in soul-jazz and lo-fi.

**Minor key progressions**:
Lo-fi also uses minor tonality heavily. In A minor:
- Am9 → Fmaj7 → Em7 → Dm7 (i – VI – v – iv)
- Am7 → G7 → Fmaj7 → E7 (i – VII – VI – V — a descending bass line progression)

**Modal / ambiguous progressions**:
Some lo-fi tracks avoid strong harmonic motion and instead oscillate between two chords with non-standard relationships. Dm9 → Ebmaj7 (a half-step relationship, also called a "side-slip") creates a floating, unresolved quality that is very lo-fi in feel.

## Chord Rhythm and Comping

Jazz chord comping is not just playing chords on the beat — it's about *when* and *how* the chords land. Lo-fi comping patterns:

**Stab and sustain**: A short chord stab (1/16 note) lands slightly before or after the beat (an 8th note early or late), followed by a sustained chord. This creates a laid-back, behind-the-beat feel.

**Anticipation**: Chords that land on the 4th 16th note of beat 4 (just before bar 2) are called anticipated chords. They push forward rhythmically without being on the beat.

**Sparse whole-note pads**: For background harmonic texture, use whole-note or 2-bar sustained chords at low velocity. These provide harmonic context without competing with the melodic elements.

In the Piano Roll, offset chord positions by ±1–3 ticks for humanization. Every note in the chord doesn't have to start simultaneously — slightly staggering them by 2–5 ticks creates a more natural roll-style articulation.

## Voice Leading Between Chords

Voice leading means moving each individual voice (note) in a chord to the nearest available note in the next chord, with minimal movement. Good voice leading makes chord changes sound smooth and connected.

**Example — Dm7 to G7 with voice leading**:
- D4 → D4 (stays, becomes the 5th of G7)
- F4 → F4 (stays, becomes the b7th of G7)
- A4 → G4 (moves down a step to the root of G7)
- C4 → B3 (moves down a half-step to the 3rd of G7)

In the Piano Roll, compare the note positions of adjacent chords and minimise movement. When voices move by more than a third, consider whether a different inversion would allow smoother movement.
keyTakeaways: Minor 7, major 7, and minor 9 chords are the core of lo-fi harmony — they create the suspended, mellow quality that distinguishes lo-fi from pop and trap.
Open and drop-2 voicings spread chord notes across two octaves for a spacious sound — avoid stacking all notes tightly in the same octave.
Good voice leading connects chord changes smoothly by moving each note to the nearest available note in the next chord, minimising large interval jumps.
lessons.tryThis: In the Piano Roll at 85 BPM, program a 4-bar chord progression using open voicings: bar 1 Dm9, bar 2 Gmaj7, bar 3 Em7, bar 4 Am7 (then repeat). For each chord, enter notes with slight staggering — each note starts 3 ticks after the previous. Set all chord notes to whole-note length so they sustain. Slightly offset the start of each chord: bar 1 on beat 1 exactly, bar 2 slightly anticipated (a 16th note early), bar 3 on beat 1, bar 4 slightly late (3 ticks after beat 1). Notice how the rhythmic variation makes the chord comping feel played rather than programmed.

lessons.title: Lo-Fi Melody and Bassline
lessons.body:
A lo-fi beat without a melody is just a drum loop. The melody — whether it's a sampled horn, a plucked guitar phrase, a Rhodes piano motif, or a filtered vocal snippet — is what makes the track memorable and emotionally resonant. The bassline provides the harmonic foundation that connects the drums to the chords and moves the progression forward.

## The Lo-Fi Melody Character

Lo-fi melodies have a specific quality: **intimate, understated, and slightly imperfect**. They don't soar or belt like pop melodies — they murmur and suggest. The typical lo-fi melody:

- Moves in small intervals (steps and small skips, rarely leaps larger than a fifth)
- Uses space — notes followed by rests, not continuous streams
- Is performed at moderate velocity (not all-out intensity)
- Often uses a pentatonic or hexatonic scale to avoid dissonance
- Has a slight imperfection in timing or pitch that suggests a real performer

## Instrument Choices for Lo-Fi Melody

| Sound | Character | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Electric piano / Rhodes | Warm, slightly bell-like | FLEX (Rhodes preset) |
| Acoustic piano | Clear but intimate when processed | FLEX, samples |
| Flute | Breathy, expressive | FLEX, samples |
| Guitar (single-note) | Organic, slightly buzzy | FLEX, samples |
| Vibraphone | Mellow sustain, jazz feel | FLEX (Vibe preset) |
| Soprano saxophone | Expressive, human | Sampled |

For any of these, the processing (from Lesson 1) is as important as the raw sound. A pristine digital flute becomes a lo-fi flute with vinyl crackle added, a low-pass filter at 12 kHz, and slight wow/flutter pitch modulation.

## Writing the Melody

The best approach is to hum the melody first, then transcribe it to the Piano Roll. Don't start by clicking notes — start by singing or humming the phrase you want, then find those notes. This keeps the melody rooted in musical intuition rather than visual grid logic.

**Melodic ideas that work well in lo-fi**:

1. **Repeated motif with variation**: A 2-bar phrase that repeats with a small change on the second iteration — a different ending note, or the same phrase starting a third higher.

2. **Question and answer**: A 2-bar ascending phrase (the "question") followed by a 2-bar descending phrase (the "answer"). The resolution of the answer gives a satisfying arc.

3. **Riff over the chord changes**: A short, repeated melodic riff (1 bar) that stays consistent while the chords change underneath. The riff sounds different over each chord even though the notes are the same.

4. **Chord tone targeting**: Each note of the melody lands on a chord tone of the underlying chord on the downbeats, with passing notes in between. This creates a melody that always sounds "in" the harmony.

## Piano Roll Techniques

**Note length**: In lo-fi, notes often sustain longer than in other genres. A melody note that sustains through the next beat (overlapping in the Piano Roll) creates legato phrasing. Conversely, short staccato notes (1/32 length) on an otherwise legato phrase create a rhythmic spike.

**Velocity shaping**: The melody should breathe dynamically. Use the velocity lane to create phrases that swell and recede. The highest velocity point in a phrase is usually the highest-pitched note (the peak of the phrase). The lowest velocity notes are the grace notes and passing tones.

**Pitch bend and vibrato**: Add subtle pitch bend on sustained notes — a slow rise of 10–15 cents over 1–2 beats, then a return. This mimics the natural vibrato of a flute or saxophone. In FL Studio, right-click a note → Properties → set pitch modulation, or draw a pitch automation curve.

## The Lo-Fi Bassline

Lo-fi bass is typically:

- **Upright bass or acoustic bass feel**: Use a sampled or synthesised upright bass sound, not an electric bass. FLEX has good upright bass presets.
- **Simple and melodic**: Follows the root motion of the chord progression with passing notes between chord changes
- **Low volume**: Lo-fi bass sits back in the mix rather than pushing forward aggressively
- **Short to medium note length**: Not the sustained, sliding 808 of trap — more like plucked notes with natural decay

**Basic bassline approach**:
1. Play the root of each chord on beat 1
2. Add a passing note (chromatic or scalar approach) on beat 4 of the bar leading into the chord change
3. Optional: add a 5th or octave jump for variety on the 3rd beat

**Example in A minor (Am7 – Fmaj7 – Em7 – Dm7)**:
- Bar 1: A2 on beat 1, G2 on beat 4 (step down leading into F)
- Bar 2: F2 on beat 1, E2 on beat 4 (half-step approach into E)
- Bar 3: E2 on beat 1, D2 on beat 4 (step down into D)
- Bar 4: D2 on beat 1, C#2 on beat 4 (chromatic leading tone back to A)

## Layering Melody and Chords

Once you have both chords and melody, check that they don't compete:

- The melody should occupy a **higher register** than the chord voicings (melody above the top note of the chord)
- If the melody note is also a chord tone, the chord reinforces it — which is good
- If the melody note clashes with a chord note (a minor 2nd interval), consider whether it's an intentional dissonance (jazz passing note) or an accident
- Use **velocity** to ensure the melody sits above the chords: melody notes at 80–100, chord notes at 50–70

The goal is a layered texture where the melody is the voice, the chords are the harmonic backing, and the bass is the rhythmic and harmonic foundation — each in its own register and volume space.
keyTakeaways: Hum or sing the melody before programming it — musical intuition produces better melodies than clicking notes on a grid without a sound in mind.
The bassline should follow chord roots and use passing notes on beat 4 to approach each chord change smoothly — think upright bass, not 808.
Keep melody at higher velocity (80–100) and chord voicings lower (50–70) so the melody floats above the harmonic background without fighting for attention.
lessons.tryThis: Using the chord progression from Lesson 3 (Dm9 – Gmaj7 – Em7 – Am7), write a 4-bar melody using only the notes D E F G A B C (D Dorian mode — same notes as C major but starting on D). Keep the melody in the register above D4. Use mostly quarter and eighth note lengths, with at least one long sustained note per bar. Then programme a simple bass: root on beat 1 of each bar, passing note on beat 4. Set melody velocity to 90 and chord velocity to 60. Play all three layers together.
